sources.notes: Count possible target rooted trees after operations on white nodes defined by even subtree size.
Detailed writeup cuts parent edges of white nodes, proves fixed components, and derives the chain-counting formula.

annotation.summary: Count the different target trees reachable when operations move even-subtree white components so all white nodes lie on one root-to-node path.
annotation.constraints: n up to 2e5, so the count must be derived from a single DFS and a closed formula.
annotation.core_idea: Cut the parent edge of every non-root white node. This partitions the tree into the root component S0 and even-sized components S1..Sk, each with exactly one top white node and fixed internal structure. The target condition requires arranging all non-root components into one chain below S0. For a fixed order, attaching component B under component A has |A|*|B| choices; summing over all orders gives a factorial/product formula involving component sizes and inverse sizes.
annotation.complexity: O(n) per test case after factorial precomputation.

solution_variants.name: white-component-chain-formula
solution_variants.summary: DFS to collect even-subtree component sizes, then evaluate the component-order formula.

tags.evidence: A DFS computes subtree parity and extracts component sizes.
The answer is a closed-form count over permutations and attachment choices of components.
The formula sums over all orders of the detached components and separates the last component choice.
tags.description: Count objects by ordering components/items and multiplying choices along the order.
tags.created_reason: Permutation-order counting is a reusable subpattern within combinatorics.

tags.evidence: The key transformation is cutting edges above white nodes to form fixed components.
tags.description: Transform a graph/tree problem by cutting or contracting into independent components.
tags.created_reason: Component decomposition is a common graph/tree reduction worth indexing.
